title: Academic Achievement: Grading/Progress Reports to Parents
description: This policy governs how Conroe ISD communicates student academic progress to parents, including the issuance of grade reports and notices for unsatisfactory performance in core subjects. It establishes requirements for parent signatures on progress documents, opportunities for in-person parent-teacher conferences, and the use of electronic delivery for notices. The policy also defines who qualifies as a parent and includes exemptions for students who meet certain independent status criteria.

timeline_local.summary: This update to Conroe ISD's EIA (Grading/Progress Reports to Parents) policy makes several legislative-driven changes: it adds a new Definitions section clarifying "parent," allows electronic delivery and signing of written notices, replaces the generic nine-week report card language with a more specific nine-week written notice requirement including parental signature, replaces the vague interim report language with a detailed statutory requirement for unsatisfactory performance notices in foundation curriculum subjects, expands the Conferences section to require at least two in-person parent-teacher conferences per year, removes the reference to "conferences scheduled on the campus calendar," and adds a requirement for the District to adopt implementing procedures.
changes_added: Definitions section header and text: 'In this policy, "parent" includes a guardian, conservator, or other person having lawful control of a student.'

Progress Reporting introductory paragraph: 'In this Section, the District may provide written notices to parent's electronically and may permit a parent to sign written notices electronically through an electronic platform, provided the District retains a record verifying the parent's acknowledgment and offers the option of a handwritten signature.'

Notices exception paragraph: 'The notices under this Section shall not apply to a student who is 18 years of age or older and who is living in a different residence than the student's parents, is married, or has had the disabilities of minority removed for general purposes.'

Nine-Week Reports section: 'The District shall, at least once every nine weeks, provide written notice to a parent of a student's performance in each class or subject and require the parent's signature on the notice, to be returned to the District.'
Performance measurement sentence: 'Performance shall be measured in accordance with this Ppolicy, District procedures, and the standards established in EIE.'

Interim Reports replacement paragraph: 'Interim progress reports may be issued at the teacher's discretion; however, the District at least once every three weeks, or during the fourth week of each nine-week grading period, will give written notice to a parent or legal guardian of a student's performance in a subject included in the foundation curriculum under Section 28.002(a)(1) if the student's performance in the subject is consistently unsatisfactory, as determined by the District. The District may provide notice for other subjects. The notice required under this section shall provide for the signature of the student's parent and be returned to the District.'

Conferences section: 'The District shall provide for at least two opportunities for in-person conferences during each school year between each parent of a child enrolled in the District and the child's teachers.'
Closing sentence: 'The District shall adopt procedures to implement this Policy.'
changes_removed: The District shall issue grade reports/report cards every nine weeks on a form approved by the Superintendent or designee.

Interim Reports original paragraph: 'Interim progress reports may be issued at the teacher's discretion; however, notice of a student's consistent unsatisfactory performance shall be issued in accordance with law.'
In the Conferences section: 'to conferences scheduled on the campus calendar,'
timeline_local.notes: The cover page title heading reads 'Academic Achievement: Grading/Progress Reports to Parents (EIA)' without specifying a reading stage (e.g., 'first reading,' 'adopt,' etc.), so the action could not be determined from the title alone; marked as 'unknown.' The rational states 'Legislative changes.' Red underlined text and red strikethrough text were clearly distinguishable throughout the document. The 'Ppolicy' capitalization anomaly in the Performance sentence appears to be a drafting artifact in the redline.

elements.element: Definitions section — 'parent' includes guardian, conservator, or other person having lawful control
elements.local_additions: The statute analysis contains no requirement or permission compelling or authorizing this specific definitional language. TEC §28.022 as quoted uses 'parent' and 'parent or legal guardian' without defining those terms. The district independently chose to codify this definition in policy.

elements.element: Electronic delivery of written notices and electronic parent signatures via district platform
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §28.022, as amended by S.B. 12 (89th Leg., R.S., effective 2025)
elements.supporting_quote: A district that uses an electronic platform for communicating student grade and performance information to parents may permit a parent to sign a notice required under Subsections (a)(2) and (a)(3) electronically
elements.litigation_status: None identified — active SB 12 preliminary injunction (S.D. Tex., Feb. 2026, covering Houston ISD, Katy ISD, Plano ISD) targets DEI, social transitioning, and curriculum provisions only; grading and notice provisions are not subject to any identified injunction as of April 9, 2026
elements.local_additions: None — policy tracks the statutory permission without expanding it

elements.element: Retention of record verifying parent acknowledgment when electronic signatures are accepted
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §28.022, as amended by S.B. 12 (89th Leg., R.S., effective 2025)
elements.supporting_quote: so long as the district retains a record verifying the parent's acknowledgment of the required notice
elements.local_additions: None — policy mirrors the statutory condition exactly

elements.element: Option of handwritten signature must be offered when district accepts electronic signatures
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §28.022, as amended by S.B. 12 (89th Leg., R.S., effective 2025)
elements.supporting_quote: A district that accepts electronic signatures under this subsection must offer parents the option to provide a handwritten signature as provided under Subsection (b)

elements.element: Notices exception — policy does not apply to student aged 18+ living apart from parents, married, or with disabilities of minority removed
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §28.022, as amended by S.B. 12 (89th Leg., R.S., effective 2025)
elements.supporting_quote: A policy adopted under this section does not apply to a student who: (1) is 18 years of age or older and who is living in a different residence than the student's parents ... (2) is married ... (3) has had the disabilities of minority removed for general purposes
elements.local_additions: The statute creates automatic exclusions operative by their own terms; no supporting quote in the statute analysis compels the district to affirmatively reproduce this language in local policy text, so codification is a permitted rather than required act.

elements.element: Nine-week written notice to parents of student performance in each class or subject, with required parental signature returned to district
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §28.022, as amended by S.B. 12 (89th Leg., R.S., effective 2025)
elements.supporting_quote: requires the district, at least once every 12 weeks, to give written notice to a parent of a student's performance in each class or subject | The notice required under Subsections (a)(2) and (a)(3) must: (1) provide for the signature of a student's parent ... (2) be returned to the district
elements.local_additions: MIXED: The obligation to issue written performance notice in each class or subject, require parental signature, and return it to the district is Required by Law. However, the statute mandates this 'at least once every 12 weeks'; Conroe ISD's policy sets the interval at 'at least once every nine weeks' — a more frequent cadence that exceeds the statutory floor and is a locally discretionary enhancement.

elements.element: Performance measured in accordance with Policy, District procedures, and standards in EIE
elements.local_additions: This sentence cross-references internal policy (EIE) and district procedures with no corresponding requirement or permission in the statute analysis. It is an administrative housekeeping provision reflecting local discretion.

elements.element: Interim progress reports at teacher's discretion (permissive provision)
elements.local_additions: The statute analysis contains no requirement or permission governing discretionary interim progress reports issued by individual teachers. This remains a local policy choice.

elements.element: Written notice to parent/legal guardian at least once every three weeks or during the fourth week of each nine-week grading period for consistently unsatisfactory performance in foundation curriculum subjects
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §28.022, as amended by S.B. 12 (89th Leg., R.S., effective 2025)
elements.supporting_quote: requires the district, at least once every three weeks, or during the fourth week of each nine-week grading period, to give written notice to a parent or legal guardian of a student's performance in a subject included in the foundation curriculum under Section 28.002(a)(1) if the student's performance in the subject is consistently unsatisfactory, as determined by the district
elements.local_additions: The policy adds 'The District may provide notice for other subjects' — this mirrors no express statutory text in the analysis and is a locally discretionary addition. The prefatory 'teacher's discretion' language for general interim reports is also locally discretionary (addressed in separate element above).

elements.element: Unsatisfactory performance notice must provide for parental signature and be returned to district
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §28.022, as amended by S.B. 12 (89th Leg., R.S., effective 2025)
elements.supporting_quote: The notice required under Subsections (a)(2) and (a)(3) must: (1) provide for the signature of a student's parent ... (2) be returned to the district

elements.element: At least two in-person parent-teacher conference opportunities per school year
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §28.022, as amended by S.B. 12 (89th Leg., R.S., effective 2025)
elements.supporting_quote: The board of trustees of each school district shall adopt a policy that: (1) provides for at least two opportunities for in-person conferences during each school year between each parent of a child enrolled in the district and the child's teachers
elements.litigation_status: None identified — active SB 12 preliminary injunction (S.D. Tex., Feb. 2026) does not encompass this provision
elements.local_additions: Removal of prior language 'to conferences scheduled on the campus calendar' reflects conformance to the statutory in-person minimum; no locally chosen enhancements beyond the statutory floor are present in this element.

elements.element: District shall adopt procedures to implement this Policy
elements.local_additions: No provision in the statute analysis requires the district to adopt implementing procedures. TEC §28.022 as quoted does not contain a 'shall adopt procedures' mandate directed at the district beyond adopting the policy itself. This is an administrative governance choice made locally by the board.

compliance_summary: Nine-Week Progress Reports: State law S.B. 12 (TEC §28.022) requires schools to send parents a written report on their child's performance in every class or subject and collect a signed acknowledgment from parents. Conroe ISD's policy sets this notice at least once every nine weeks, which is more frequent than the state minimum of once every 12 weeks.

Unsatisfactory Performance Notices: Under TEC §28.022 as updated by S.B. 12, schools must notify parents in writing at least once every three weeks — or during the fourth week of each nine-week period — when a student is struggling in core curriculum subjects. The policy also requires parents to sign and return those notices to the district.

Electronic Signatures and Delivery: S.B. 12 allows schools to send required notices and collect parent signatures electronically through a district platform, instead of only using paper. If a district accepts electronic signatures, state law requires it to also offer a handwritten signature option and keep a record confirming the parent acknowledged the notice.

Parent-Teacher Conferences: TEC §28.022, as amended by S.B. 12, now requires schools to offer parents at least two in-person parent-teacher conferences each school year. The updated policy reflects this requirement and removes older language that tied conferences to the campus calendar schedule.

Locally Added Definitions: The policy includes a section defining 'parent' to cover guardians, conservators, and others with legal responsibility for a child. This definition was not required or specifically authorized by state law — the district chose to add it for clarity.

Procedures Requirement: The updated policy directs the district to create internal procedures to carry out the policy's requirements. State law does not specifically require this step; it is a local administrative decision made by the school board.

timeline_local.summary: This update to Conroe ISD's EIA (Local) policy adds three new requirements: (1) parents must sign and return grade reports every 12 weeks, (2) interim progress reports for consistently unsatisfactory performance must also require a parent signature and be returned, and (3) parents must be provided at least two in-person conference opportunities per school year with their child's teacher. All signature requirements include an exemption for students 18 or older living independently, married, or with disabilities of minority removed.
changes_added: The District shall require a parent's signature on the report, to be returned to the District every 12 weeks. This shall not apply to a student who is 18 years of age or older and who is living in a different residence than the student's parents, is married, or has had the disabilities of minority removed for general purposes.

If a student's performance in the subject is consistently unsatisfactory, as determined by EIE(Local), the student's interim progress report shall require a parent's signature and be returned to the District. This shall not apply to a student who is 18 years of age or older and who is living in a different residence than the student's parents, is married, or has had the disabilities of minority removed for general purposes.
A student's parent shall be provided with at least two opportunities for in-person conferences during each school year between themselves and their child's teacher.
timeline_local.notes: The cover page does not contain a standard TASB action title heading (e.g., "Discuss Revisions to..." or "Adopt Revisions to..."). The document is labeled Version 1 with no date issued, and the cover page title simply reads "Academic Achievement: Grading/Progress Reports to Parents (EIA)" without a procedural action phrase. Action has been set to 'unknown' as a result. All three red-underlined additions were clearly identifiable. No red strikethrough (removed) text was present in the document.

description_local: This policy covers updates to five different local school district policies, all revised in response to recent changes in Texas law, addressing topics ranging from employee legal protections and student grading practices to medical treatment, crisis intervention, and child abuse reporting. The changes affect a broad range of people connected to the school community, including teachers and other staff, students, and parents. Parents and community members should be aware of these updates because they directly impact how schools handle student grades and progress reporting, respond to health and safety emergencies, protect children from abuse and neglect, and support the legal rights of school employees.
